title: Straw pulp sheet forming from agricultural waste fibre
subtitle: Making rough, warm-toned paper from wheat straw or reed
excerpt: Before wood-pulp paper, straw and reed were widely used papermaking fibres in regions without abundant trees. A straw-pulp sheet is rough, warm-buff in colour, and distinctly textured. The preparation is more involved than cotton: straw must be cooked to break down its silica and lignin: but the resulting paper has a character that cotton cannot replicate.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain, Internet Archive. Hunter documents straw and esparto grass as historical European papermaking fibres widely adopted in the mid-nineteenth century before the dominance of wood pulp.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of simmering plant material in a sodium carbonate (washing soda) solution to dissolve lignin and silica, separating the cellulose fibres for papermaking.

attrs.heading: Safety: soda cooking
attrs.body: Sodium carbonate (washing soda) solution at cooking temperatures is caustic; use rubber gloves when handling hot cooking liquids. Work outdoors or in a well-ventilated space; the steam from cooking straw smells strongly. Do not use the cooking pot for food after use.

content.text: Straw cannot be blended directly into pulp because its silica content prevents the fibres from separating cleanly.
breaks down the silica and lignin, leaving behind cellulose fibres that can be blended. The process takes 2-3 hours of simmering plus rinsing time, so plan for a half-day session.

items.name: Wheat straw or dried reed, 200 g
items.description: Harvest after the growing season; the drier the better. Cut into 5 cm lengths. Straw from a farm or garden supply; avoid treated straw (fertiliser-treated straw produces a darker, less clean fibre).

items.name: Sodium carbonate (washing soda), 40 g
items.description: Sold as a laundry product. Dissolve in 2 litres of water in a non-food pot.

items.name: Non-food cooking pot, 4-litre minimum
items.description: Stainless steel or enamel. Reserve this pot for fibre work only.

content.text: Add the cut straw to the sodium carbonate solution in the pot. Bring to a gentle simmer and maintain for 2 hours, adding water if the level drops below the straw. The straw will soften, lose its rigidity, and the liquid will turn a deep amber-brown. After 2 hours, remove the pot from the heat and let it cool completely before handling. Pour the cooking liquid down an outside drain; do not pour it into a septic tank.

Rinse the cooked straw thoroughly in several changes of cold water until the rinse water runs clear. The fibres are now soft enough to blend. Place a small handful in the blender with 600 ml of water and blend for 15-20 seconds. The slurry will be warm buff or straw-coloured. Add to the vat and form sheets as for cotton linter.

Straw sheets dry to a warm buff-tan colour that darkens slightly over time. The surface is rougher than cotton and the sheet is slightly brittle compared to long-fibre materials. It takes brush, pencil, and pastel well but bleeds with watercolour unless heavily sized. The historical use for straw paper was newspaper, packaging, and inexpensive writing paper; for craft use it works best as a background material or as a mixed-fibre element (30% straw, 70% cotton linter gives a sheet with colour interest and reasonable strength).
